pages.raw_text:
Members of the various labor
unions not allied with the Bar
tenders union openly express in
dignation at the stand taken by that
union against the seating of Rev.
w. J. Hindley as fraternal dele
gate from the Ministerial associa-
ton. The labor men say that they
are now represented before the pub
lie, in a measure, as the allies of
the saloon element of the city.
They further state that the action
of the local bartenders will do
more to create sympathy for the
clergy with all classes than any
thing that could have happened and
will do much to bring about a
closed town.


c. o. Young, district organizer OF
the American Federation of Labor
speaks highly of the work being
done by the clergy for the better
ment of labor. He says that the





and other cities are working har-
moniously with organized labor at
Olympia for the enactment of an
eight hour law for working women.
The Seattle ministers investigated
conditions in that city in depart
ment stores. restaurants and laun-
dries and found that the half had
not been told of the deplorable con-
ditions existing among the girls and
women employed in those establish
ments, and it was clergyman who
presented to the state federation
the resolutions adopted by the Sc
attic ministers urging the enact
ment of the shorter work day.


The bartenders STATED that they
would withdraw from the Central
Labor union unless the clergy were
kept out. and were to be followed
it was stated, by the cooks and
kitchen helpers and the walters and
mattresses, in all about 500 men
and women.


1t is stated by local union men
that the international laws of these
unions would compel them to again
affiliate with the central body
should they withdraw. This law, il
is further stated, originated in this
city and was supported at the inter
national convention by the presi
dent of the local Bartenders
union.


Delegates to the Central Labor
union from the local unions are be
ing criticised by members of their
unions for allowing the delegates
from the Bartenders' union to bull.
